metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the coverage-dict contract for the new verify-command lint: define the key name(s) under which per-line abstain information is recorded (e.g., `verify_lint_abstained: int`, `verify_lint_checked: int`) so AC-2 can assert on a concrete shape and downstream coverage consumers remain compatible.
findings.finding: The abstain-aggregation protocol is stated in prose ('per-line abstains must aggregate into [the coverage dict], not per-line events') but the aggregation key names, value types, and accumulation rule are never defined. DetResult.coverage is typed as dict[str, Any] with no enumerated keys (confirmed at det_floor.py:87). The decision rule is thus not co-located with the schema it must satisfy, leaving the implementer to invent both the key names and the accumulation semantics.
findings.location: What section, sentence beginning 'NOTE: p6_ac_quality emits ONE DetResult with a flat coverage dict'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the input source for the verify-command lint: state `ctx.state['verify_commands'][*]['command']` entries, inline proof/command strings extracted from AC item text, or both — and add a sentence in the What section explaining which channel is primary.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify which input source(s) the verify-command lint processes: the structured `ctx.state['verify_commands']` list (VerifyCommandEntry objects with a `command` key, set via `set_verify_commands`) or inline proof/command strings extracted from AC item text (e.g., the `Proof: pytest …` lines visible in the plan). These are two architecturally distinct channels and the defect pattern ('a present command that silently lies') is most directly observable in the AC item text, not necessarily in the state store.
findings.location: What section ('Flag three defect classes'); AC line 1 ('p6_ac_quality extended with the verify-command lint'); det_floor.py line 514 (existing `ctx.state.get('verify_commands')` read)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC3 (e.g., `grep -n 'shellcheck' det_floor.py | grep -i 'adopt\\|bespoke\\|justify'`) and replace the open-ended '(adopt or justify bespoke)' with an explicit decision: either commit to shellcheck integration or to bespoke regex, and state the minimum content the comment must contain.
findings.finding: AC3 requires 'shellcheck prior-art note recorded in the criterion's code comment (adopt or justify bespoke)' but provides no proving command — compliance is not automatically verifiable, and 'justify' has no objective standard, making this criterion effort-framed rather than outcome-measurable.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC3 with: 'shellcheck prior-art decision recorded as a one-line comment in det_floor.py at def p6_ac_quality, stating either "shellcheck adopted: <reason>" or "bespoke: shellcheck not suitable because <reason>". Proof: `grep -c shellcheck det_floor.py` ≥ 1 and the plan author has pre-committed to one of the two forms above.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a canonical detection spec for defect classes (2) and (3): define which grep-pattern forms count as 'unanchored' (e.g., pattern argument not starting with `^` or `\b`) and enumerate the metacharacters and syntactic rule for 'unquoted' (e.g., `$`, `` ` ``, `!`, `*`, `?`, `[` outside single- or double-quotes) so the fixture can be written to an objective, externally verifiable definition.
findings.finding: Defect classes (2) 'unanchored-grep patterns' and (3) 'unquoted shell metacharacters' are named but their detection vocabulary is never defined — no specification of which grep-pattern forms count as unanchored (^ / \b / both?), nor which metacharacters are in scope, nor what syntactic context counts as 'unquoted'. These function as an implicit enum whose value boundaries are unspecified, making the AC-2 fixture tautological rather than a genuine correctness check.
findings.location: What section, defect class bullets (2) and (3)
findings.suggested_fix: Add two-line specs in the What section: '(2) unanchored-grep: a grep/egrep/rg call whose pattern argument does not start with ^ and contains no \b; (3) unquoted-metachar: a shell word containing $, `, !, *, ?, or [ that is not enclosed in single-quotes and not a double-quoted variable-only expansion — detection via character-class scan after stripping quoted regions.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the inline 'do NOT add a P10' directive with a positive constraint statement (e.g., 'the extension must be implemented as an addition inside p6_ac_quality, not as a new DET_CHECKS entry') to avoid the pink-elephant antipattern; the hard-assert in test_det_invariants.py is the enforcement mechanism.
findings.finding: The What section embeds a negative-constraint instruction ('do NOT add a P10') directly in the plan body, which is a pink-elephant / instruction-locality antipattern. This primes any LLM or human executor to foreground the prohibited action rather than encoding the constraint as a testable invariant (which AC-1 partially does, but the inline instruction duplicates and emphasises it in a way that can be read as a directive).
findings.location: What section, parenthetical: '(det_floor.py def@493). Consider shellcheck as prior art before a bespoke regex set.'
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite as: 'Extend p6_ac_quality (det_floor.py:493) in-place; DET_CHECKS must remain P1–P9, enforced by the existing hard-assert in test_det_invariants.py:208.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact detection criteria for defect classes (2) and (3): specify which grep-pattern forms count as 'unanchored' (e.g., pattern not starting with ^ or \\b) and enumerate the shell metacharacters in scope (e.g., `$`, `` ` ``, `!`, `*`, `?`, `[`) and the syntactic rule for 'unquoted' — so the fixture and implementation can be written to an objective spec.
findings.finding: Defect classes (2) 'unanchored-grep patterns' and (3) 'unquoted shell metacharacters' lack a precise technical definition — the executor must invent detection criteria (which anchors? which metacharacters? in what syntactic context?) without a specification to target.
findings.location: What section, defect class bullets (2) and (3)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a two-line spec under each defect class, e.g.: '(2) unanchored-grep: grep/egrep call whose pattern argument does not start with ^ or contain \\b; (3) unquoted-metachar: shell word containing $, `, !, *, ?, or [ outside single- or double-quotes, detected via a simple tokeniser or shellcheck --format=gcc output parsing (if shellcheck is adopted).' This removes executor discretion and makes the fixture deterministic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a lightweight extension point for defect-class patterns (e.g., a `_VERIFY_LINT_RULES` list of `(name, matcher_fn)` tuples inside det_floor.py) so WS5/E6 can add rules without modifying p6_ac_quality's control flow.
findings.finding: The three defect-class patterns are hardcoded within p6_ac_quality with no defined extension point, yet the plan explicitly anticipates future expansion (WS5/E6 will add richer taxonomy). Without a structured registration mechanism or data-driven pattern list, adding new defect classes later will require surgical edits to the same function body.
findings.location: What section — 'EXTEND p6_ac_quality (det_floor.py def@493)' / AC2 fixture set
findings.suggested_fix: Replace inline if-branches with a small `_VERIFY_LINT_RULES: list[tuple[str, Callable[[str], bool]]] = [...]` registry at module level. Each entry is `(defect_class_name, predicate)`. p6_ac_quality iterates the registry and records hits/abstains per rule. WS5/E6 appends to the registry without touching the loop.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the shellcheck adopt-vs-justify decision IN THE PLAN (not deferred to a code comment): either (a) confirm shellcheck is inapplicable because verify-command text is prose/not a parseable script and document that rationale, or (b) specify that shellcheck runs on extracted code-fence content in fail-open subprocess mode — so the implementation choice is explicit before coding begins.
findings.finding: The plan proposes a bespoke three-pattern regex taxonomy for shell-command linting while acknowledging shellcheck as prior art, but defers the adopt-vs-justify decision entirely to a post-hoc code comment (AC #3). The codebase already uses shellcheck (infra/gerrit/*.sh, scripts/) and ships ast-grep via grounding/engine_b.py. No rationale is provided in the plan for why bespoke regex is preferred over invoking shellcheck on extracted code-fence content (even in fail-open/subprocess mode), leaving the NIH risk unresolved at plan-approval time.
findings.location: ## What (verified constraint) — defect classes (1)/(2)/(3) + AC #3 'shellcheck prior-art note recorded in code comment'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Shellcheck rationale' sub-bullet under ## What: e.g. 'shellcheck is not invoked because verify-command text in plan prose is not a parseable shell script; the three regex patterns cover the mechanically-checkable subset that does not require a full parser. If a future story extracts fenced code blocks, shellcheck should be preferred over regex extension.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a precise operational definition for each of defect classes (2) and (3) in the What section: for (2) specify which anchoring forms are required (e.g., 'grep/egrep call whose pattern argument does not begin with ^ or contain \b'); for (3) enumerate the metacharacters in scope (e.g., `$`, `` ` ``, `!`, `*`, `?`, `[`) and the syntactic rule for 'unquoted' (e.g., 'shell word not enclosed in single- or double-quotes') — so the fixture and implementation can both be written to an objective spec.
findings.finding: Defect classes (2) 'unanchored-grep patterns' and (3) 'unquoted shell metacharacters' have no operational definition — no specification of which anchoring forms count as 'unanchored' (e.g., pattern not starting with ^ or \b?) nor which metacharacters in which syntactic contexts count as 'unquoted'. Without a canonical definition the fixture can be written to pass whatever the implementation produces, making the AC tautological and providing no real correctness guarantee.
findings.location: What section, defect class bullets (2) and (3); AC line: 'Fixture set covers ALL THREE defect classes (one command each)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add two-line specs under each bullet, e.g.: '(2) unanchored-grep: a grep/egrep call whose pattern argument does not start with ^ or contain \b; (3) unquoted-metachar: a shell word containing $, `, !, *, ?, or [ that is not wrapped in single- or double-quotes, detected via a minimal tokeniser or shellcheck SC2086/SC2046 rule reference.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the shellcheck capability question before implementation: verify whether `shellcheck -s bash -` (stdin mode) can lint single-line verify-command strings and map its output to the three defect classes, then record the concrete outcome (e.g., 'stdin mode works but requires subprocess + pinned version, ruled out for fail-open purity' or 'stdin mode cannot isolate defect class 1, bespoke regex adopted') in the AC-3 code comment rather than leaving the trade-off open.
findings.finding: The plan's shellcheck prior-art note creates an implicit unverified capability assertion: that shellcheck is a viable alternative for linting individual verify-command strings extracted from ticket state. Shellcheck is designed to lint shell script files or full stdin scripts — not isolated command strings. Applying it to single-line commands (e.g., 'grep foo bar.txt') would require a subprocess harness, tmpfile/stdin wrapping, version pinning, and a mapping from shellcheck's SC-code taxonomy to the plan's three defect classes. None of this is verified, and shellcheck is not a project dependency (absent from .pre-commit-config.yaml and pyproject.toml). The AC's 'adopt or justify bespoke' obligation therefore risks being trivially discharged ('shellcheck can't lint strings → bespoke') without surfacing whether shellcheck's stdin mode (shellcheck -s bash -) could actually replace the regex approach.
findings.location: What section / AC-3: 'shellcheck prior-art note recorded in the criterion's code comment (adopt or justify bespoke)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief note to the plan (or AC-3) stating the result of testing `echo 'grep foo bar' | shellcheck -s bash -`: if it catches unanchored patterns and missing assertions, justify the subprocess cost; if it doesn't map to all three defect classes, state that explicitly so the 'justify bespoke' arm of AC-3 is pre-grounded.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
